titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Critical Indigenous Photographic Exchange Collection
titlestmt.author: by Matthew Daniel Mason

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Saturday February 27, 2021 at 04:07

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: Portraits created by Will Wilson of Native American artists, arts professionals, and leaders as well as others, 2012-2018. Wilson created the images using a wet plate collodion process that produced tintypes on sheets of aluminum. He then gave the tintypes to the sitters after he created high-resolution digital scans of the objects. The 303 inkjet prints in the collection derive from the digital scans of the tintypes.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: Portraits created by Will Wilson of Native American artists, arts professionals, and leaders as well as others, 2012-2018. Wilson created the images using a wet plate collodion process that produced tintypes on sheets of aluminum. He then gave the tintypes to the sitters after he created high-resolution digital scans of the objects. The 303 inkjet prints in the collection derive from the digital scans of the tintypes.

Wilson titled, signed, and dated inkjet prints on rectos in English, except for the series, PHOTO/SYNTHESIS , which has inscriptions on the versos of the inkjet prints. All prints additionally designated "Beinecke Edition."

Throughout the collection, Wilson uses abbreviations to designate portraits made at particular sites. These abbrevations include "CIPX" for Critical Indigenous Photographic Exchange and then designations for the site such as "NMMA" for New Mexico Museum of Art in Santa Fe, New Mexico.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Peters Projects, Llc, on Walter McClintock Memorial Fund, 2017. Purchased from Will Wilson on Walter McClintock Memorial Fund, 2018-2019.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Critical Indigenous Photographic Exchange Collection is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Will Wilson, Critical Indigenous Photographic Exchange Collection. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, as well as more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit. This guide derives from a detailed examination of each print. Dates in the collection reflect the date of image capture.

bioghist.head: Will Wilson (born 1969)
bioghist.p: Will Wilson is a Diné photographer. He studied photography at Oberlin College (Bachelor of Arts, Studio Art and Art History, 1993) and the University of New Mexico (Master of Fine Arts in Photography, 2002). Since 2014, Wilson has supervised the photography program in the School of Arts, Design and Media Arts at Santa Fe Community College, New Mexico.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into twelve series: I. Santa Fe Studio, 2012. II. New Mexico Museum of Art, 2012. III. Santa Fe Art Institute, 2012. IV. Institute of American Indian Arts, 2012. V. Denver Art Museum, 2013. VI. School for Advanced Research, 2013. VII. Maxwell Museum of Anthropology, 2013. VIII. Red Dot Gallery, 2015. IX. Portland Art Museum, 2016. X. PHOTO/SYNTHESIS , 2016. XI. Spoken Through Clay , 2016. XII. Seattle Art Museum, 2017. XIII. Tempe Center for the Arts, 2018. XIV. Santa Fe Community College, 2018.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created by Wilson at his studio (CIPX SFe Studio) in Santa Fe, New Mexico, 2012, and printed as inkjet prints, 56 x 43 centimeters (22 x 17 inches).

title.part: William Wilson, Citizen of the Navajo Nation, Trans-Customary Artist

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Print includes blue tinting applied to turquoise stones in his necklace.

title.part: Bruce Bernstein, United States Citizen, Director of the Southwestern Association of Indian Arts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also designated in the inscription by Wilson as part of CIPX NMMA .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created by Wilson at the New Mexico Museum of Art (CIPX NMMA) in Santa Fe, New Mexico, August 2012, and printed as inkjet prints, 56 x 43 centimeters (22 x 17 inches).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter.

title.part: Kathleen Ash-Milby, Citizen of the Navajo Nation, Curator at the Smithsonian National Museum of American Indian
Nicholas Galanin, Citizen of the Tlingit Nation, Trans-Customary Artist
Joe D. Horse Capture, Jr., Citizen of A'aninin Indian Tribe of Montana, Associate Curator of Native American Art, Minneapolis Institute of Art
Terrance Houle, First Nation Citizen of the Blood Tribe, Interdisciplinary Media Artist
Zig Jackson, Citizen of the Mandan, Hidatsa, and Arikara Nation, Professor of Photography, Savannah College of Art and Design
Cara Romero, Citizen of the Chemehuevi Indian Tribe, Program Director of Indigeneity, Bioneers
Gaylord Torrence, United States Citizen, Senior Curator of American Indian Art, Nelson-Atkins Museum

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created at the Santa Fe Art Institute (CIPX SFAI) in Santa Fe, New Mexico, 2012, which consist of portraits of cast members of Dancing Earth, an Indigenous contemporary dance ensemble, and printed as inkjet prints, 56 x 43 centimeters (22 x 17 inches).

title.part: Eric Lopez Garcia, Citizen of the Tarasco First Nation, Dancer, Dancing Earth

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inscription identifies him as "Eric Lopez Garcia." He is also known as Xeric Meraz.

title.part: Jack Gray, Ngāti Porou and Te Rarawa, Founder of Atamira Dance Collective, Aotearoa, New Zealand

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Misdated 2016.

title.part: Raven Knight, Citizen of the Jicarilla Apache Nation, Dancer, Dancing Earth
Sandra Lamouche, First Nations Citizen of the Bigstone Cree Nation, Dancer, Dancing Earth

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One print misdated 2016.

title.part: Nakotah Larance, Citizen of the Hopi Tribe, Six Time World Champion Hoop Dancer
Nimkii Osawamick, Citizen of the Ojibwe First Nation, Wikwemikong, Manitoulin Island Unceded Reserve, Dancer, Dancing Earth

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created at the Institute of American Indian Arts (CIPX IAIA) at Santa Fe, New Mexico, 2012, and prinetd as Inkjet prints, 56 x 43 centimeters (22 x 17 inches).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter.

title.part: Gerald and Lynn McMaster, Gerald is a citizen of Siksika First Nation, Curator of Canadian Art at Art Gallery of Ontario
Beverly Morris, Aleut Corporation, Documentary Filmmaker, Independent Curator
Ronald J. Solimon, Citizen of the Pueblo of Laguna, Director, Center for Lifelong Education, Institute of American Indian Art

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created at the Denver Art Museum (CIPX DAM) at Denver, Colorado, April 2013, and printed as inkjet prints, 56 x 43 centimeters (22 x 17 inches).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter. In cases with multiple sitters, the surname of the person appearing earliest in the alphabet is used for arrangement.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created at the School for Advanced Research (CIPX SAR) at Santa Fe, New Mexico, September-November 2013, and printed as inkjet prints, 56 x 43 centimeters (22 x 17 inches).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter. In case with multiple sitters, the surname of the person appearing earliest in the alphabet is used for arrangement.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created at Red Dot Gallery (CIPX RDG), Santa Fe, New Mexico, 2015, and printed as inkjet prints, 60 x 43 centimeters (23.5 x 17 inches) except where noted.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter.

did.unittitle: Inkjet Prints, 43.5 x 56 centimeters (17 x 22 inches)

scopecontent.p: Images created at Portland Art Museum (CIPX PAM), Portland, Oregon, 2016, and prinetd as inkjet prints, 61 x 43 centimeters (24 x 17 inches) except where noted.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter. In cases with multiple sitters, the surname of the person appearing earliest in the alphabet is used for arrangement.

scopecontent.p: Identified by Wilson as Mia Whitley.

title.part: Demian DinéYazhi, Artist, Activist, Heterogeneous Homosexual, Citizen of the Navajo Nation
Brandy Zubano, United States Citizen
PHOTO/SYNTHESIS

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created of Native Americans in Oklahoma as a response to Edward Curtis, North American Indian , Volume 19, The Indians of Oklahoma: Cheyenne, Comanche, Ponca, Wichita, Osage, Pawnee and Otoe (Norwood, Massachusetts, The Plimpton Press, 1930). The images formed part of the exhibition, PHOTO/SYNTHESIS (CIPX PSYN) at the Fred Jones Jr. Museum of Art, University of Oklahoma, Norman, Oklahoma.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Inkjet Prints, 30.5 x 30.1 centimeters (13.25 x 12 inches) and Inkjet Prints, 56 x 44 centimeters (22 x 17 inches).

did.unittitle: Inkjet Prints, 33.5 x 30.5 centimeters (13.25 x 12 inches)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes prints with measurements, 30.5 x 30.5 centimeters (13.25 x 13.25 inches)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter. In cases with multiple sitters, the surname of the person appearing earliest in the alphabet is used for arrangement.

scopecontent.p: Print measures 30.5 x 30.5 centimeters

title.part: Charlotte Niyah McCurtain, Citizen of the Comanche Nation, Full Blood

scopecontent.p: Print measures 30.5 x 30.5 centimeters

scopecontent.p: Print measures 30.5 x 30.5 centimeters

title.part: Shannon Raylene (Sovo), Citizen of Comanche Nation , 1997 Comanche Nation Princess, Descendant of Big Looking Glass, and Gene A. Sovo, Citizen of Comanche Nation and Affiliated Mohawk, French
Ann Marie Woolworth, Citizen of Cheyenne and Arapaho Tribes, Age 13, Basketball Player, Grandparents are Woolworth and Madbull
Charles Woolworth, Citizen of Cheyenne and Arapaho Tribes, Age Eight, Baseball Player, Grandparents are Woolworth and Madbull

Eibel Yellowfish, Citizen of the Otoe-Missouria Tribe and Affiliated Comanche, Osage, Muscopee (Creek), Caddo, Pawnee, Sac and Fox, Delaware, Chickasaw, Potawatomi, "Munje Thigbe," Age Five, and Reiquen Yellowfish, Citizen of the Otoe-Missouria Tribe and Affiliated Comanche, Osage, Muscopee (Creek), Caddo, Pawnee, Sac and Fox, Delaware, Chickasaw, Potawatomi, "Wah-shinga-sahpi" (Blackbord), Age Five, Descendants of DeRoin, Moove, Yellowfish, Green, Oosahwe Families
Cristina "Cricket" Hart Yellowman, Citizen of Cheyenne and Arapaho Tribes
Gordon L. Yellowman, Citizen of Cheyenne and Arapaho Tribes

did.unittitle: Inkjet Prints, 56 x 44 centimeters (22 x 17 inches)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes prints with measurements, 30.5 x 30.5 centimeters (13.25 x 13.25 inches).

Images designated as "Talking Tintypes" allowed viewers to scan the prints with the camera of smartphone or tablet computer using the Layar App, an augmented reality browser that then played a digital video of the subject discussing their experiences.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter. In cases with multiple sitters, the surname of the person appearing earliest in the alphabet is used for arrangement.

title.part: John A. Barrett, Tribal Chairman, Citizen of Potawatomi Nation, "Keweoge" (He-Leads-Them-On), Citizen Potawatomi Nation Families: Peltier, Bonrossa

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Talking Tintype.

title.part: Deidre Bigheart, Citizen of Osage Nation and Affiliated Cherokee, "Wah-Kan-Sa-Moi" (Walking Wind), Great-Great Granddaughter of John Wah-She-Wah-Hah Bigheart and Her Children, and Kyle Varley, Citizen of Osage Nation and Affiliated Cherokee, "Wah-Tse-Da" (Guiding Star) and Annawake Varley, Citizen of Osage Nation and Affiliated Cherokee, "Hum-Pa-Tokah" (Wet Moccasin), and Alec Bigheart, Citizen of Osage Nation and Affiliated Cherokee, "Wah-Tse-Khi-He-Kuh" (Star Chief)
Terril Rae Bigheart, Citizen of Osage Nation, Great Granddaughter of John Wah-She-Wah-Hah Bigheart
Courtney Burgess, Citizen of Otoe-Missouria Tribe and Affiliated Iowa, Chickasaw, Sac and Fox, Otoe Bear Clan, Tribal Treasurer, Burgess and Kihega Families

Bunky Echo-Hawk, Citizen of Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma and Affiliated Yakama, Artist, and Jasha Lyons Echo-Hawk, Citizen of Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma and Affiliated Seminole, Creek, Omaha, Iowa, Justice Seeker
Kevin Gover
Enoch Kelly Haney, Citizen of Seminole Nation of Oklahoma, Artist, Former State Senator, and Principal Chief of Seminole Nation of Oklahoma

Catherine Moore, Citizen of Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma, Descendant of Colonel Moore and Dollie Moore, and Wilson Moore and Virginia Moore, and Marsha Pauline Elaine Hawkins, Citizen of Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma, Daughter of Mary Hawkins, and Chelsie Nicole Baldwin, Citizen of Iowa Tribe of Kansas and Nebraska, Founder of Healing Centers

Casey Camp Horinek, Citizen of the Ponca Tribe of Oklahoma, "Zhutni," Tribal Councilwoman, Leader of the Ponca Scalp Dance Society, Sundancer, Delegate to the United Nations Permanent Forum of Indigenous Issues, Matriarch of Wonderful Family (Grandmother, Companion, Mother, Sister), Defender of Mother Earth
William "Bill" H. Howell, Citizen of Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma, Full-Blood
Terri Parton, Citizen of Wichita and Affiliated Tribes and Affiliated Caddo, President of Wichita and Affiliated Tribes
Thomas Ryan RedCorn, Citizen of Osage Nation, Artist
Modesto Schonchin, Citizen of Comanche Nation and Affiliated Klamath, Modoc, Paiute, "Tubitsi" (Real Truthful), Age Nine

Hallie Winter, Citizen of Osage Nation, "Wah Kansa Moie," Great-Great-Great Granddaughter of WahSheWahHah, and Cindra Shangreau, Citizen of Osage Nation, "Hum Pa Tokah," Great-Great Granddaughter of WahSheWahHah

Chairman John Shotton, Citizen of the Otoe-Missouria Tribe and Affiliated Iowa, Otoe-Missouria Owl Clan, Descendant of Jim Devoine and Lina Kihega DeRoin, Frank Moore, and Mary Dailey Moore, Edna DeRoin Moore, Bob Moore, Orissa Shotton Moore

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Talking Tintype.

Brielle Turney, Citizen of the Comanche Nation, Descendant of Chiefs Mugura and Aruka-Papi

Mary Jo Watson, Citizen of Seminole Nation of Oklahoma, Director Emeritus, School of Art and Art History, Regents' Professor

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Talking Tintype.

title.part: Spoken Through Clay

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Portraits of Native American potters published in Charles S. King, Spoken Through Clay: Native Pottery of the Southwest: The Eric S. Dobkin Collection (Santa Fe, New Mexico: Museum of New Mexico Press, 2017).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created at Seattle Art Museum (CIPX SAM), Seattle, Washington, November 2017. Inkjet prints, 61 x 43 centimeters (24 x 17 inches).

Images designated as "Talking Tintypes" allowed viewers to scan the prints with the camera of smartphone or tablet computer using the Layar App, an augmented reality browser that then played a digital video of the subject discussing their experiences.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images created at Tempe Center for the Arts (CIPX TCA), Tempe, Arizona, February 2018 and printed as inkjet prints, 56 x 43 centimeters (22 x 17 inches).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by the surname of sitter. In cases with multiple sitters, the surname of the person appearing earliest in the alphabet is used for arrangement.
